title: Breathwork Triggered a Kundalini Crisis: What Holotropic Breathing Actually Does

body:
Breathwork Triggered a Kundalini Crisis: What Holotropic Breathing Actually Does

You're here, aren't you? It's probably late. You've been scrolling, searching, desperate for an answer. Maybe you just did some breathwork. Maybe it was "holotropic." And now you're feeling things you can't explain, things that are terrifying. You're not sleeping. Your body feels like it's vibrating, buzzing, burning. Your mind is racing, flashing images, memories, insights you didn't ask for. You're questioning everything. Your sanity. Your life. You feel alone, like no one could possibly understand this hell you're in. Trust me, I get it. I've been there. I know that particular brand of fear that hits you when your reality shatters, and you're left picking up the pieces, wondering if you'll ever be whole again. Or if you even want to be whole again, because what was whole anyway? It's a mess, right? A total fucking mess.

I'm Kalesh. I went through it. Kundalini crisis, triggered by breathwork, just like you. I'm not a doctor, I'm not a guru, and I'm not here to sell you anything but my experience. I'm here to tell you what I learned, what I wish someone had told me at 3 AM when I was convinced I was losing my mind, or worse, dying. This isn't pretty. It's not a spiritual retreat brochure. This is raw. This is what it actually feels like, and what you might be dealing with.

The Setup: Why You Even Tried Breathwork

You probably started breathwork for good reasons. Stress, anxiety, seeking something more, right? You heard it was good for you. It's trendy. Everyone's doing it. "Open your chakras," "release trauma," "connect with your higher self." Sounds great on paper. You found a workshop, an online guide, maybe a guided meditation. The instructor probably said it was totally safe, just a little intense. "You might feel some tingling," they said. "Some emotions might come up." They didn't mention your entire nervous system might get rewired without your consent. They didn't mention the possibility of feeling like you're being electrocuted from the inside out, or that you might spend months in a state of existential dread, questioning every single belief you ever held. Did they? No. Because most people don't know. Most people leading these sessions haven't gone through it themselves, not really. They've read books, they've done a weekend certification, and they're repeating what they've been told. They don't know what happens when you accidentally kick open a door that you're not ready to walk through.

Holotropic breathing, specifically, is designed to induce non-ordinary states of consciousness. It's not just deep breathing for relaxation. It's meant to bypass your usual filters, your ego, your conscious mind, and bring up unconscious material. It's a powerful tool, no doubt. Stan Grof, who developed it with his wife Christina, studied psychedelic therapy for years. He knew the power of these states. He developed holotropic breathwork as a way to access similar experiences without the substances. That's a key piece of information, isn't it? You're basically giving yourself a non-drug psychedelic trip. And just like with psychedelics, sometimes you have a bad trip. Sometimes that trip just keeps going, and going, and going. You can find out more about his work, and the experiences of others, in The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link). It's an important book, honestly, if you're trying to make sense of what's happening.

What Happens to Your Body During Holotropic Breathwork

When you breathe rapidly and deeply for an extended period - that's hyperventilation, know what I mean? - you start to change your body's chemistry. You're taking in a lot of oxygen, and you're blowing off a lot of carbon dioxide. This changes the pH of your blood, making it more alkaline. This is called respiratory alkalosis. What does that mean for you? A whole bunch of things. It constricts blood vessels, including those in your brain. Less blood flow to the brain can cause lightheadedness, dizziness, visual disturbances, and altered perception. It can also cause muscle spasms, tingling, numbness, especially in your hands and feet - that's called tetany. Your hands might curl up into a weird claw shape. Your jaw might clench. Your body might shake uncontrollably. All of this is a direct physiological response to the breathing. It's not necessarily "energy moving." It's your body reacting. But here's the kicker: these physical sensations can be interpreted by your mind as something else entirely. Something spiritual. Something energetic. Something deep. And that's where the confusion starts.

The Psychological Impact: Opening the Floodgates

Beyond the physical, there's the psychological. When you're in that altered state, your mind's defenses are lowered. Memories, emotions, traumas that you've suppressed for years can come flooding back. Sometimes it's clear, sometimes it's symbolic, like dreams but more vivid. You might re-experience past events with intense emotional clarity. You might feel a sense of connection to something larger than yourself, or a deep sense of terror. You might see visions, hear sounds, feel presences. For some people, this is incredibly healing. They process old wounds, gain new perspectives, feel lighter. For others, it's like opening Pandora's box without any idea how to close it. The material that comes up is too overwhelming, too intense, too destabilizing. And if you're prone to anxiety, depression, or have a history of trauma, you're at a higher risk of having a difficult experience that you can't easily integrate. This isn't a game. You're messing with your consciousness, your very sense of self. Right?

Kundalini: The Uninvited Guest

So you've done the breathwork, and things feel... off. Really off. This is where Kundalini enters the picture. What even is Kundalini? In spiritual traditions, it's often described as a dormant energy, coiled at the base of your spine, like a serpent. When awakened, it's supposed to rise up through the chakras, purifying your system, leading to enlightenment, spiritual awakening, cosmic consciousness. Sounds amazing, doesn't it? The ultimate spiritual upgrade. But here's the thing: you don't just "wake up" Kundalini with a couple of breathwork sessions and then go back to your normal life. When it truly activates, it's a force of nature. It's a biological process, a psycho-spiritual transformation that takes over your entire being. And it doesn't always go smoothly. In fact, for many, it's anything but smooth.

When Kundalini awakens suddenly or violently, especially without proper preparation, guidance, or a stable foundation, it can manifest as what's called a Kundalini crisis or spiritual emergency. This isn't enlightenment. This is hell. This is your nervous system being overloaded, your psyche being completely dismantled, and your body feeling like it's on fire. It's not just "tingling" anymore. It's intense heat, burning sensations, electrical currents shooting through your body, involuntary movements, spasms, tremors, vibrations. Your heart might race, you might feel pressure in your head or spine. You might have visions, hear internal sounds, experience deep shifts in perception, memory, and identity. You might feel like you're going crazy. And you're not alone in feeling that. Many people have written about this, like in The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). It's a classic for a reason.

I'll tell you what, when it hit me, I thought I had a brain tumor. Or multiple sclerosis. Or some rare neurological disease that doctors had never seen. I went to every specialist imaginable. They all said I was fine. "Anxiety," they said. "Panic attacks." They didn't understand. How could they? The sensations were so real, so visceral. It wasn't just a thought, it was my entire body convulsing, vibrating, feeling like it was going to explode. The mental anguish was even worse. Dissociation, derealization, feeling like I was watching my life from outside my body. Intense fear, paranoia, moments of absolute bliss followed by crushing despair. It was a rollercoaster from hell, and I couldn't get off.

Why Breathwork is a Trigger

So, why would breathwork, specifically, trigger this? Because it directly impacts your nervous system and your energy body. Kundalini is often described as a neurobiological process. It's not just some abstract spiritual concept. It involves your brain, your spine, your endocrine system, your entire physiological makeup. Rapid, deep breathing, especially when combined with intention, focus, and an expectation of a deep experience, can act as a powerful catalyst. It floods your system with oxygen, changes your blood chemistry, and creates a state of physiological arousal that can mimic or directly initiate a Kundalini awakening. It's like pouring rocket fuel onto a fire you didn't even know was there, or worse, onto a fire you weren't ready to contain. It can bypass the gradual, natural unfolding that's often seen in more traditional, slower spiritual practices. You're basically forcing the issue, and your system might not be ready for that kind of intensity. It's like trying to run a marathon without training. You're going to crash, hard.

Look, I'm not saying breathwork is by nature bad. For some, it's a gentle, restorative practice. For others, it's a way to access deep states of healing. But you need to understand its power. You need to understand that it's not just a casual exercise. It's a tool that can deeply alter your state of consciousness and potentially activate powerful psycho-spiritual processes that you are not equipped to handle on your own. It's like playing with fire, and sometimes, you get burned. Badly. And that burn can last for years.

What to Do When You're in Crisis

Okay, so you're in it. You're feeling everything I just described, and you're probably freaking out even more now. That's okay. Take a breath. A slow one. Not the fast kind. Here's what I wish someone had told me:

  • First, rule out medical issues: I know, I know, you've probably done this. But if you haven't, go see a doctor. Get a full check-up. Tell them everything, even if you think they'll think you're crazy. Rule out anything physical. This isn't about ignoring your body; it's about making sure you're physically safe.
  • Acknowledge what's happening: Stop fighting it. The more you resist, the worse it gets. This isn't a "bad trip" you can just snap out of. This is a process. It's happening. And it's going to take time. Acceptance is the first step, no matter how hard it is. You're not going crazy, you're going through something real, even if it feels unreal.
  • Ground yourself: This is crucial. Your energy is probably all over the place. Get back into your body. Eat nourishing food. Go for walks in nature. Touch the earth. Take a warm bath. Listen to calming music. Engage your senses. Anything that brings you into the present moment and out of your head. Avoid stimulants - no caffeine, no sugar, no alcohol, no drugs. Seriously. They'll only make it worse.
  • Seek support, carefully: This is a tricky one. Most doctors won't understand. Most therapists won't either. You need someone who understands spiritual emergency. Look for therapists or coaches who specialize in transpersonal psychology, spiritual emergence, or Kundalini integration. There are networks out there, like the Spiritual Emergence Network (SEN). They're not easy to find, but they exist. Don't just talk to anyone. You need someone who gets it, who won't pathologize you or try to medicate away a spiritual process. This book, Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link), is another excellent resource for finding support and understanding what's happening.
  • Simplify your life: Seriously. Get rid of anything that adds stress. Reduce your commitments. Take time off work if you can. Don't try to force productivity. Your system is undergoing a massive overhaul. You need rest. You need quiet. You need space.
  • Focus on self-care: This isn't selfish; it's survival. Gentle yoga, meditation (but be careful with intense practices - sometimes less is more when you're overwhelmed), journaling, creative expression. Do things that soothe you, that bring you even a tiny bit of peace.
  • Educate yourself: Read everything you can about Kundalini, spiritual emergence, and transpersonal experiences. The more you understand, the less terrifying it becomes. Knowledge is power, especially when you feel powerless. The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is another great place to start.
  • Be patient: This is not a sprint. This is a marathon, and it can last months, even years. There will be good days and bad days. There will be moments of clarity and moments of utter confusion. Don't expect a quick fix. Just focus on getting through each day, each hour, sometimes each minute.

You're not broken. You're going through an intense process of transformation. It's messy, it's terrifying, and it feels like you're being torn apart. But on the other side of this, there's a new you waiting. A more authentic, more aware, more compassionate you. You just have to survive the journey. And you will. I know you will. Because I did. And if I can do it, so can you. Hang in there. You're not alone in this dark night. Does that land?

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
